nativeRange.habitat: Damp shaded surfaces in subtropical and tropical Asian forests; grows on rocks, tree trunks and submerged or emersed wood in or near streams. Reaches noticeably larger frond dimensions than the related Fissidens fontanus (Phoenix Moss).

decorPreferences: driftwood (especially branchy pieces that showcase the fern-like fronds)
porous rock, lava rock, Ohko/dragon stone or seiryu
stainless or plastic mesh sheets for moss walls and carpets
tied with cotton thread / fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
never buried in substrate — the base will rot if covered
gentle to moderate flow keeps debris off the feathery fronds

goodWith: tetras, rasboras and other small community fish

summary: An Asian-native epiphytic moss with distinctive fern-like fronds and a noticeably larger leaf-pocket structure than the related Phoenix Moss (F. fontanus). Bright green, slow-growing, undemanding under low light, and should be attached to wood or rock rather than buried.
careNotes: Fissidens nobilis is an Asian-native bryophyte distinguished from the smaller, more common Phoenix Moss (F. fontanus, fw-plant-068) by its larger and more strongly fern-like fronds. Like other Fissidens, it is an EPIPHYTE and must be attached to a hard surface — driftwood, porous rock (Ohko/dragon stone pairs particularly well aesthetically), lava rock, or plastic/stainless mesh — and NEVER buried in substrate, where the base will rot. Tie with cotton thread or fishing line, or use cyanoacrylate gel; rhizoids anchor over several weeks and the binding can then be removed. LIGHTING is low to medium; the species thrives in low-tech tanks and shaded areas without supplemental light, and intense lighting is not required. CO2 is not required — Modern Aquarium and the broader Fissidens literature treat injection as optional, with denser, more compact growth in CO2-supplemented tanks. GROWTH RATE is slow (some hobby sources rate it slow to moderate); slow growth is generally an advantage for detailed aquascaping. SIZE is roughly 3–8 cm in height per frond, larger than F. fontanus (typically 2–5 cm), which is the main morphological distinguishing feature in the trade. TEMPERATURE tolerance covers a typical tropical/subtropical aquarium window of 18–25 °C (65–77 °F per Modern Aquarium); stability matters more than the exact target. pH 6.0–7.5, soft to moderately hard water (gH 2–12, kH 0–8) is inferred from the closely related F. fontanus and standard Asian-stream biotope conditions. FERTILIZATION demand is low — the moss feeds from the water column and does not require nutrient-rich substrate; aggressive chemical dosing can damage delicate fronds. FLOW: gentle to moderate keeps the fine fronds clean; very weak flow lets debris accumulate in the fern-like leaf pockets and should be avoided or compensated for with regular gentle turkey-basting during maintenance. SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: the dense, branching habit makes excellent microhabitat for dwarf-shrimp larvae and newly hatched fry. SOURCING NOTES: The originally-assigned Buce Plant URL (buceplant.com/products/fissidens-nobilis) returned HTTP 404 at fetch time and was abandoned per the playbook's primary-failure rule; Modern Aquarium was used as the manifest primary, with Wikipedia confirming family/order/class taxonomy at the genus level. F. nobilis is not specifically listed in the Wikipedia Fissidens article's species selection, but Fissidentaceae/Dicranales/Bryopsida/Bryophyta are uniform across the genus and applied here.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only under aquarium conditions: separate any healthy fragment from the mat (the trade product is often shipped on a thread mat that can be unwound into clumps) and re-attach it to a new piece of hardscape. Every viable fragment regrows. Trim with aquascaping scissors and remove loose fragments before they drift onto other plants. True sexual reproduction (sporophyte formation) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in fully submerged aquarium tanks.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium — Fissidens Nobilis Mat
primary.notes: Used for: care level (beginner/easy), lighting (low), CO2 (not required), growth rate (slow to moderate; recorded as slow), placement (foreground accents on rocks/driftwood; epiphyte), temperature 65–77 °F (18–25 °C), mat dimensions (~3in x 3in / 7.6 cm), fern-like feathery texture, debris-trapping caveat with very weak flow, propagation by separating the mat into clumps, shrimp-friendly habitat note, decor pairings (Ohko/dragon stone, driftwood).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Fissidens (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy-only source per the playbook source whitelist. Used for family (Fissidentaceae), order (Dicranales), class (Bryopsida), division (Bryophyta) and genus-level morphology (vaginal lamina; palmately or pinnately arranged leaves). Wikipedia's species selection does not specifically list F. nobilis, but the family/order applies uniformly across the genus.

propagation: fragmentation (any healthy frond regrows when re-attached)
trim with aquascaping scissors to shape and densify
